Welcome to week two of the Momentum Builder Workshop Lean Edition. This week is going to focus on that point of first contact with our prospects, which is a Facebook ad. Just to refresh our memory, let's look at the overview of what we will be covering in this workshop. On the left we have prospects, those prospects have desires, fears, things that they need to accomplish. Those things that they need to accomplish have functional elements, they have social elements, and they have emotional elements. We're going to take them through a journey. First, by building awareness with the Facebook ad, then we're going to take them through a landing page that's going to have the ability for them to opt in. Then we'll take them through a series of relationship building emails and finally a series of promotional emails. And what we hope happens at the other side of this journey is that we are producing happy customers. And because we are arranging this entire journey around a single thematic arc or a single controlling idea, we're amplifying the power of our messaging. We're giving ourselves a home base to return to when we're creating all of these various elements. And it's that sense of congruence that really matters. This week I want to share with you something that's probably one of the most powerful tools that Andre and I use. It's difficult to explain and I'm going to take my best shot at this and we'll explain it in further detail as well in the written content for this module. This is probably one of the most powerful things that we use. To do that, I need to change the orientation of how we're looking at this thematic arc. So instead of looking at it left to right, what I want you to think about is that now we're looking, we've just turned it, it's the same journey, everything's exactly the same, but now we're looking at it more face on. And that helps. It seems like that doesn't really matter. But it helps us think about this, that this is not this sort of left to right thing that's happening. It's very much we're seeing it or prospects are seeing it through their own eyes and it's a step-by-step journey that they're taking. And it's really appearing in front of them. If you think about it every day, they're getting an email after they've signed up or they've seen an ad, then they go see landing page. So it's these series of sequential steps in front of them. It also helps that this makes the next visual a little bit easier too. So this visual is the frame. And I want to make sure that when I talk about the frame, if you are, if you're in the durable business and we use the term, the frame for how we're writing, that's, we're not talking about the same thing here. And if you saw an email we wrote about our writing process several weeks ago or a month or so ago, it's not, it's, it's not the same thing. The frame in our writing, it's the same word, but it's a different concept. And we've actually never shared this concept before. So I'm really excited, maybe a little bit nervous about it too. So what's a frame? Neil Strauss has written many books, defined a frame as a frame is the context or belief through which a person's situation or a conversation is perceived. What does that mean? Well, think about it when you're in the doctor's office and the doctor comes in and he or she gives you whatever news, everything looks fine. Blood tests are great. We're not quite sure about something. What's the frame? Well, the white lab coat, the fact that that person went to medical school, all of that establishes a frame where the doctor is the expert. So when the doctor comes in and says, blood test looks great, you don't say, well, how do you know that? What makes you think that? The, the frame has already been said, the doctor's the expert in whatever field he or she is practicing in, you're there to get the expert's opinion. A frame can be lots of different things. A frame really tells us what's possible and what isn't possible. The frame for Harry Potter is that there is a world where magic exists, right? That's the frame. Everything, if that, if you don't accept that frame, nothing in Harry Potter makes sense. You know, if you, if you watch Star Wars, what's the frame for Star Wars? Well, the frame is that there are these, there's this ancient order of lawyers called Jedi who are at odds with this, sort of the light and darkness. There's, that's the frame. And if you accept that, and it's a world where space travel is possible and where different creatures exist, like all of that is created by the frame. And anything that's within that frame is possible and anything that's outside that frame is impossible. The way we'll use this, we're obviously not writing the next Harry Potter or the next Star Wars, but we want to use the same idea. And what we're, we're doing in this, in this multi-step journey is we are bringing prospects into a new reality, our reality. And that may be showing them that something they didn't believe was possible is possible. There are lots of them. We'll go, I'll go through some examples in a moment, but everything is set by the frame. And once you establish that frame, then, and it happens early. And if we don't get it right early, and we're not very clear about it early, then everything that follows is muddy. Because we don't know if it's, if it's part of the reality that we're entering in, or if it is, if it's not included, it's just very difficult to think about, like what, what's actually going on here. So let's look at some examples. I think it'll be a lot easier to understand frames when we can think about them from an example standpoint. So first one we'll start off very near and dear to my heart. I've mentioned in week one that I really love the expert and the insider theme. So how does the frame work with the theme? Because to be clear, these two are very much related. And I don't want this to become an intellectual exercise where everybody's confused, like, well, what's the theme versus what's the frame? So for the sake of this example, in the previous week, I mentioned the expert insider theme. I like that. I think anyone who can pull that off, it makes a lot of sense. And I'm also drawn to people who have put the work in in their various fields. And I like to be around experts in different fields. So that's part of it. That's the broader theme. Expertise, there's an insider view. That's the broader theme. But it becomes a frame really through that point of first contact where you're establishing a reality. So with the traffic engine in early 2020, we want Andre and I together launched the traffic engine. And the traffic engine was really the culmination of my 21 years at the time, running a digital agency that specialized and paid traffic. At the time, I'd managed somewhere around $100 million in ad spend across every imaginable business entity you can possibly imagine. I'd worked with a lot. I've worked with a tremendous number of clients and lots of different fields, many of whom you'd recognize their name. And the frame that was set in that initial conversation that we described about the traffic engine, when we said this thing is coming, the frame that we set was Andre and I have been friends for a long time for 18 months, we've been talking about doing this, creating this course. And we hadn't and I realized we hadn't because for me, it felt like a RM and sphere of influence were the gold standards in sort of in general. And there was a tremendous amount of weight for me to create a course that could be at the level of autoresponder madness and sphere of influence. So all of that came together to set this frame that it did thinking out loud here, that frame did a lot of things. One, it was my way of showing respect to Andre's work and to position Andre's work where I believe it deserved to be, which is that these are the gold standards in our field. And that it also positioned my attempt to create a course to be at that same level. So that everything that followed about the traffic engine was seen through that initial frame of wanting of taking 21, 22 ish years of expertise and putting it into something that I wanted to become the gold standard, much like autoresponder madness and sphere of influence. So if you read that and you started and you said you raised your hands that I'm interested in this, tell me more. What you're doing is you're accepting the frame that we created. You're stepping into that frame and we have now established the boundaries of reality. And it's a very subtle agreement that the person viewing makes or the person reading makes and the person creating makes it's an agreement that happens. Now, if I had said something that was not true, or if I had said ridiculous made ridiculous claims, you know, I you can this is not there's no guarantee that by setting a frame people will buy into it. It helps and it's critical for the for the expert insider theme. You can't just make this stuff up. I'm trying not to have turned out to use an example that I really want to use. I won't name the person. I don't know him, but there's a Facebook ad running that I see in my news feed occasionally. And it's somebody claiming to be he does a really interesting persuasion trick and he says some people have said I'm the and then he can and he compares himself to a legend in his field. So he's setting the frame. If you if you start to go down that road, you're you're, you're believing you're accepting that this person, you know, some people really say this person is the next you know, master of whatever. So and just by the looks of the photos and stuff, I think the young man's in his 20s or something. So I don't I don't want to disparage anybody, but I'm pretty sure he's not at the level that he thinks he is or suggesting he is. So I mentioned that only because if you're going to use the expert or insider frame theme and when you frame it in the beginning and if you talk if you're thinking about really setting yourself up as the expert and then the frame talks about your two or three years in the field, that's not congruent. People are going to pick up on that unless you know maybe some feels two or three years gets you there. I don't know. Let's look at some other examples. Counterintuitive theme. We've talked about 80 20. Richard Kosh's work on 80 20 is a great example of a counterintuitive frame. So what Richard Kosh does, as he says, you know, what if you if you want to to get more out of life, more success, more money or whatever, conventional wisdom says that you need to work harder on and on and on. But the reality is you actually need to work less because the few things matter with those things matter a lot. That's the foundational idea of 80 20. When you buy into 80 20, the world gets framed in such a way where you're not looking for all of the things that you could do. You're looking for somebody to explain to you the very few things that really matter. Tim Ferriss has made a career out of this. He's written many books about this, the four hour work week. That's the math behind 80 20. That was his initial his initial entry point. And that's that's really what what made Tim Ferriss Tim Ferriss in the beginning was the four hour work week. That was a counterintuitive frame. And he did it in a couple of different ways. One of them was you can work four hours and and he's since been clear about it that the idea was never that you could just work four hours. It was that that sort of conceptually you didn't have to work nearly as hard to get the results that you wanted. But what he also did in the four hour work week is he he showed the math of what a really great lifestyle could be. And it wasn't a lot of money. And that's a counterintuitive frame as well. If you if you bump into somebody on the street and they're thinking to myself I got to work the next 10 years and really kill myself to make up enough money to do the things I want to do, make enough money to do the things I really want to do. And Tim Ferriss said no, no, no, you don't understand. You should be doing the things that you want to do now. Don't delay the vacations and the fun until you're old and retired. Do it now and here's how you can do it because it's not that expensive. Like that's that's changing the frame. Now you when you when you and when you buy into that, you're you see the world differently. And that's what happens when somebody sets a powerful frame is that you begin to see the world differently. Get rich quick is a classic one in certainly an internet marketing. It's been around forever. I'm sure there was you know, back in the days of the Romans, there were probably people who had get rich quick schemes. Right. So what's what's the frame for get rich quick in general? It's, Hey, look at me. I'm loaded. Hey, look at my buddies. They're loaded to we make a ton of money because we know a few things that you don't know. And if you just pay us whatever 997 9777 whatever it is. But if you give me some money, I'll tell you the things that I did to become rich because all you need to know are these few things and that and what's the difference between not being rich and being rich is just knowing these few things. Right. Or doing them into writing. There's there's so many variations of get rich quick. Sometimes it's unique to know certain things, a certain method, a certain certain system. There are a million variations of get rich quick, but it's all the same essential frame, which is there's some that there's the possibility of not having to work very hard, not having to build up have built up any expertise, not having to really focus too much on value, that there's something that you can do. There's some way to to rig the system or the systems already rigged and you just need to know how it's rigged. And once you know that, riches are on the other side of it. Right. And that that parts so many people from their money over and over and over because people want the print they want the premise so to be true so badly. And because there's so much desire for it to be true, it's a lot easier to persuade something persuade people that something that really on on its face value is nonsense. Right. There's there's no we don't really believe in other parts of the like we don't going back to our doctor example before we don't believe that there's some way to become a doctor. That if you just knew the right two or three things to do in the right order, hey, look at me, I've got I became a doctor overnight. We don't believe it anywhere else, but somehow and get rich quick. We've we've believe it. And I think that's just because people want it to be true so badly. I'll use a quick example from something Andre and I created when we created the durable business. We were very specific about choosing that word durable. And we wrote a 10 part email series about it. Because we wanted it to be we wanted to be clear that it wasn't just any type of business. This was not a flash in a pan. Put an offer out. Make some cash and then move on to the next thing we we framed the entire thing the frame for the that entire campaign was on this idea that what you it's not just a business that you want. It's a business that's going to stand the test of time that's going to get better over time that's going to become more durable over time that's going to last like that was a frame. And it's to be clear, it's something we both believe it's easy. And I guess this is a this was an unplanned comment, but I think it's a good opportunity. I've created an opportunity for myself to mention it. Frames aren't tricks. They can be. And some of our hesitation with sharing thoughts about frames is that they can be abused, frankly, and we see them abused like we all of us together. We see them abused all the time. So when we talk about using frames like with the traffic engine or durable business or other things. Don't think about that in some nefarious way because we're using we're setting these frames because that's what we believe. And the best frame for you to think about is it's exactly that. What is it that you believe about something that or that you've come to believe about it? Maybe you didn't in the past and what the school of hard knocks, what did it teach you? How have you over time evolved and you're thinking about something that that becomes a frame? That's a perfect segue into the next one, which is why I sort of jumped ahead quickly here, which is linear versus systems thinking for a very long time in my career. I can't speak for Andre because we haven't actually had this conversation, but for a very long time in my early career, I believed in the linear funnel concept, which was if you and it used to go something like this, if you if you sent traffic, if you had an ad to a landing page, landing page to an opt in an opt into an offer, if you could double conversion at each of those steps, you would 16 X your total outcome. So you would do it in, I believe it was in reverse order right to left. So if you could double your conversion rate for sales, then you would go to the next step and double conversion rate for whatever that next step was. And then all the way back. And I think it was four steps, 16 X returns. And I, I tried every permutation imaginable for that. And it never happened. It just didn't, we, you know, we would see some interesting things happen over time, but it wasn't, it just, it never actually really made sense because the things that you would do later in a four step funnel could be offset by things you did earlier. Like it just never really worked. And what I finally stumbled onto systems thinking, all of a sudden, everything made sense that it's not any single element of a system. It's not just your ad. It's not just your landing page. It's not just your order form that there's no single element that by itself is responsible. Yet all of them together, particularly the relationships between them are what actually matter. And that it's those relationships where you have in some congruence. And that's really why we've structured this workshop the way we have is because it's not about any of these individual steps, even though it is, but it's not because it's how they all work together to produce a result. So if we replaced one of these, say, for example, for this week's example, if we created a really click baity Facebook ad, we could dramatically increase the number of clicks we got to the landing page. But most likely we would actually diminish the overall results. We would probably reduce the number of happy customers that we would get. So we would make an improvement to the local optima. So we would have the we get more clicks on the ad. So that in a linear thinking way would make it would make it look better. Yet we would undermine the system optimum, getting happy customers. So if you buy into that idea, and just having this conversation, I'm setting a frame in this conversation. I'm saying that there is there in what's what's unpacked what I've done. I've shown that there are two ways to think about getting results linear versus systems thinking. And I've shown that one from my experience is more reflective of reality. So if you're if you were a prospect in this conversation, and you're nodding your head up and down, and you're stepping toward me like tell me more, I want to I want to know more about this, you're stepping into the frame that I have now established, right? And it's not again, it's not an affair. It's the I these are things I believe this is what my experience is. I don't know that it's capital T truth. But it's reflective of my experience of reality. And I believe it there's there's some larger truth. So as you embrace it, you are stepping into the frame that I've created. Next example, TOC theory of constraints. This is Ellie Goldrat's work. Ellie Gold this gold rats work is a really great example of essentially creating an entire worldview. He has created a frame that if when you when you step into Goldrat's world, the entire everything looks different. So theory of constraints, it's it's based on systems theory, it's it's a type of systems theory. But Goldrat's idea was like, we boil it down that in any complex system, there is one and only one constraint holding back the entire systems performance. And they developed a tremendous number of tools to figure out what that was. And then he talked about you would elevate the constraint meaning you would he developed initially in a manufacturing environment. So you would find out the thing that was governing the performance of the entire system and then divert your attention and your resources to making that more capable. And then figure out where the constraint would move in the system. Because once you've addressed one, oftentimes it shows up someplace else. So theory of constraints that Goldrat has written many books, his first book was written as a parable. It's called the goal. The goal takes you into a new world. You come out the other side of reading that book, a different person, you see the world in a very different way. Because you have stepped into the frame that Goldrat has created. A couple of more quickly, throwing stones at conventional wisdom is a great frame to use. I've used a weight loss example in the past. That's an easy one to use. If there are ways that people typically do things in any field, how do people learn acquire new skills? How do they lose weight? How do they get stronger? How do they get faster? Whatever it is, if conventional wisdom has some issues with it, known or unknown, you can throw stones at those things and say, you see this happen a lot with Western medicine, where it'll be okay, if Western medicine is so great, then why do we have all of these problems still? We go through the rates of heart attacks and dementia in Alzheimer's. You go through this whole list, like that's throwing stones at conventional wisdom. That's saying that the way we think about being the right way to do something probably isn't the right way, or it's certainly not ideal. This is a really easy one to use. If you are in a field where there is a generally accepted way to do it, and if that generally accepted way to do it doesn't get particularly consistent or particularly excellent results, then this is a throwing stones at conventional wisdom. It sets a really interesting frame. It's hard to describe the frame without knowing the topic. The frame that you're setting really is specific to your industry. I'll use Jonathan Baker quickly, just as an example, just because I'm familiar with, as he explained, how people tend to go, to think about going faster on a bicycle. These are competitive cyclists, so we're not talking about recreational people. Competitive cyclists who want to go faster on a bicycle. One way they do it is they shave off weight by buying really expensive stuff, whatever those components are. I don't know that that's conventional wisdom in that field. I don't know enough about it, but that could be something that you could really throw stones at. Let's figure out all the three or four ways people do crazy stuff to go faster on a bicycle. Let's look at how much it costs and then what the results are. Now you've set this frame that says the way most people do things to try to get this result that they want makes no sense. That's throwing stones at conventional wisdom. Another one, which is something Andre and I really love, is throwing stones at BS in your market. In many ways, we're making a career out of this, but there's so much nonsense in internet marketing. It's so much of the get rich quick nonsense. There's so much stuff about all you need is just the right funnel. It's nonsense. If Andre and I could distill everything we've learned over the last four plus decades into one idea, it would be that there's no causal relationship between a tactic and an outcome. There's no causal relationship between the software that you use and the result that you get or the funnel structure that you create and the outcome that you get. That's not the nature of the business. When people are out there who are saying, if you just buy this thing, it's going to lead to riches or if you just do my course and we'll show you the exact system we used to do XYZ, the implication being that's a great frame too. I'll sell you. Let's think about this frame. I'm going to sell you my exact system I used to go from whatever, $0 to $100 million in sales. The implication is you could use that system too and that's nonsense. Absolute nonsense. It's really easy to throw stones at the BS in your market. Your markets may be different, your audience is maybe different, but this is a really easy one that sets a frame that really says most of the way things are done in whatever your feel is. Most of them are nonsense. Let me explain how. Let me show you and you don't have to show everything, but you can just throw a few stones early because it's not the individual stone that you're throwing. That's the frame. The frame is that what they've been told about this market is not actually true. You're taking out some of the foundation of the beliefs and saying this thing you've been led to believe not necessarily true. One more. I call this a black swan. I'm not trying to borrow Nassim Talaab's idea. I just like this idea about black swans. For the longest time, swans were only white. Then one day somebody saw a black swan, which means black swans can exist. The moment there's one black swan, black swans exist. The way I think about this, and this is a very powerful frame, which is if one person can do something, then others can do it too. The way you could use this frame, as you can do something like, and it's implied. You're not saying this outright, but the implication is if I pulled myself up from my bootstraps and accomplished X, then you can too. One person's done it, another person can do it. Sometimes you can even say that in the establishing of the frame. You can even say that this is one that being fully transparent works. To say, I was brought up to believe whatever X, Y, and Z, that I was never going to make anything of myself, that life was hard, that people only got rich, if they had the right connections or family money or whatever it is. I believe that for a really long time, except something happened, whatever you fill it in for you. Something happened, and I realized, and I started to think maybe I could do it. Maybe the things that I'd been told weren't entirely true. I tried, and I failed, and then I tried again, and I failed again, but after whatever, five years, 10 years, 10 tries, whatever it is. Finally, I realized that whatever, and I succeeded. If I can do that, that means you can do that. It means anybody can do that. Now that's a frame. Because the person who's hearing that, if the moment they take a step toward you, they're taking a step into this world, where the frame that's been set is that something seemed impossible, but this person did it, which means it's possible for them, and they're not that different than I am, which means it's possible for me. That's a really powerful, powerful frame. So let's switch gears here. What does this all have to do with the Facebook ad? So the Facebook ad is the point of first contact. It's where someone who has no idea who we are is going to be first exposed to whatever it is that we're the journey that we're taking them on. And from that perspective, that means the primary job of a good Facebook ad is to establish the frame for the entire customer journey. Let's just think about that for a moment. Somebody has, is in their Facebook news feed, and an image catches their attention, perhaps, or something catches their attention. And the hook, the first two lines, maybe there, we'll talk about that, but there's something in there that grabs their attention. And do you have 500 to 800 words? I mean, if you want to go to 1000, that's fine. These are not hard set formulaic limits. I would prefer that you not go much less than 500. And 800, you know, that's a that's a pretty that's a good Facebook ad. So in 500 and 800 words, we're not going to establish the entire thematic arc. We're not going to be able to tell somebody where they're going, what it's going to be like when they get there. None, we don't have the space to do that. But what we need to do instead is we need to set the frame in which the entire rest of the journey is going to happen. We need to draw some lines around the door that they're stepping into, and get them nodding up and down, like, well, that sounds really interesting, because whatever we've created in that ad that resonates with them enough, or they resonate enough with that, that they want to go forward. They want to see what what's on the landing page, they want to take that next step. They're doing that from within the frame that we've created for them. So there are two other jobs that the Facebook ad needs to do as well. The obvious one is we need to attract the right prospects, but just as important, we need to repel the wrong prospects. And I can't stress this enough, what conventional throw some stones at conventional wisdom here for a moment. Conventional wisdom states that the job of the Facebook ad is to get the click and that is bullshit. The job of the Facebook ad is to get the customer. The customer, yes, he or she has to click to eventually become a customer. But we don't want people clicking on ads to find out what's on the landing page, because we've made something curious or some word trickery, we don't want that because it's a false signal. It's meaningless, it's going to mess up all of our other data points. Because what we want is to put we want our ad to establish a frame that the person who we can become a happy customer is shaking his or her head up and down. Yes, thinking I that's that's it. That's why I haven't been successful in the past or I never thought about it that way. And now that I think about it that way, so much makes so much more sense. I'll give it a quick example of a frame that this is something that I've been subject to was a frame somebody. I stepped into somebody else's frame. And I believe it's accurate. But there's a company called Z health, their website is zhealtheducation.com. Just to be clear, I have no financial or the relationship with Z health. But this is the the work of someone named Dr. Eric Cobb. I do not know him personally. Seems like an incredibly, just a brilliant practitioner, brilliant person. But what Z health, the over I will probably butcher this, but just broadly speaking, Z health perspective is that our brain's primary responsibility is to keep us safe. And because of that, if we are trying to do things physically, particularly in an athletic setting, and we don't have the strength or the balance or other things, visions not quite right in some areas, whatever it is, that our brain will not let us put ourselves in too much danger. So it will throttle our performance. So as a lifelong martial artist, I have had experiences of flexibility, one day, total lack of flexibility, the next high performance one day, complete poor performance, the next, I've had these experiences that nothing else ever explained. But Z health did. So the when I was first exposed to Z health's idea, when the frame was set, the frame was essentially and I want to be clear, I don't, I don't know that Z health is thinking in terms of frames. But the way they frame that the way they frame the idea was the brain is keeping you safe. And when you're when you're seeing a degradation in your performance, you don't need to stretch more, you know, like there's a million things you don't need to do. What you need to do is focus on the things that cause that your brain interprets as a threat. And that can be things like lack of balance, lack of strength, vision, like a whole list of things, they have an entire curriculum around this, and I'm not doing it justice. But that frame that it that it's my brain governing my behavior, I was nodding up and down, and I've since become a significant customer of them spent a lot of money with Z health because the frame that they established that I agreed to made a lot of sense to me. In many ways, it was throwing stones at conventional wisdom. But I I have since adopted that frame, that's how I think about movement, I inhabit their frame, I'm within it, I'm not. And I like to think I could, I can still see it, if, but it's I'm much more in it than I am observing it. And that's really what we want to have happen. Now, using that same example, if I were a some practitioner of some sort of movement practitioner, and I believe that my modality was the only way to get results. And I, I would, and I would never pay somebody to pursue something different. I just believe my it's my way is the right way. And that's it. I want the ad, that Facebook ad to make me go away. I want that person looking say, I think that's nonsense, not to click through not I don't I don't want to cast open the doors so wide and say, come on in, everybody, we've got something for all of you. That's not it at all. What we want to do with the Facebook ad is we want to say, here's this world that you can now inhabit. And here's what it's going to be what's going to happen next, etc. But if this isn't like for whatever reason, if this isn't right for you, we want those people to go away. We're not trying to get some target conversion rate for our opt-ins or anything. We're not optimizing for that. We're optimizing for happy customers. And the number of every time we turn away somebody as a prospect who's not actually going to become a happy customer, we're doing our job really well. It sounds counterintuitive. Sounds crazy, actually. And this is when I was when I initially presented part of how I think about Facebook ads at Todd Brown's top one, and when the marketing idea of 2019, I've got the award around here, so I can't remember what it's called, basically the big marketing idea for that group, and presented it later in different groups and other places. I was inadvertently using a counterintuitive frame. I wasn't thinking about that really at the time. I was just trying to share something that had been working very well for me. But that's it's a good example of a counterintuitive frame because what you think you want are lots of clicks. What you actually want are lots of customers. And the things that you do to get lots of clicks actually undermine the things that you would do to get happy customers. That's a very, that's a textbook counterintuitive frame. I hadn't even thought about it until just now. And that's another, again, I want to keep emphasizing that there's authenticity and there's integrity in this. I want all of us to understand this. We are not trying to game the system. We're not trying to make up something. What we're trying to do is we're trying to apply some tools to the reality that's already true for you, for the things that you already do well, for things that you would like to do well or would like to do better. We're applying tools to that, but we are never, ever, ever trying to game the system so that we get a result that's not in line with what we're actually able to do. So the frame is not a hack. It's not a trick. It's just a way of thinking about how we're structuring the journey that's going to follow. And I like the visual, because once we start thinking about writing the ad and going on this journey in the thematic arc, I like to be able to think about what are the parameters of the frame, right? What's true in this world? And that's the big question, right? At this point, we're 39 minutes in. It's a great idea intellectually, but how in the world are we supposed to do this, right? I get it. So let's talk about lots of different ways. This is not a formula. I'm not even sure this rises to the level of a framework. What I want you to think about here is creating a Facebook ad that has these four different ingredients, and you're going to make a you're going to mix and make these ingredients into a different cake than I would, depending on your market, depending on your personality, depending on the tone that you have, all of these things are going to make a different, it's just going to be different for what you create versus what I might create versus what one of your colleagues might create. But I think if you have these four ingredients, and what and when we talk about so what they look like, I think what you're going to find is that you're going to produce, you're just a really good ad is going to emerge from the other side of that. So let's talk about the first thing. What do they want? They are your, it's your prospects, but it's a subset of your prospects. What do you, the subset are the prospects who are able to become happy customers. That's the caveat. It's not just what all your prospects want. You put 100,000 people in a group, and they're sort of loosely connected. And like, okay, what does everybody want? You make a list of 25 things, that's not going to work. What you want to do is you want to have, imagine this large audience, but within that are the prospects who can become really happy customers. They're the people you can help the most, either to get what they desire, to alleviate a problem they're dealing with, to accomplish something that they haven't been able to accomplish consistently or can accomplish well, whatever it is, that's the person you're focusing on. And when we talk about what do they want, it's really, I want you to focus on two things. What are the outcomes that they want? They can wave a magic wand, what would be true as it relates to whatever it is that you're selling, etc. So if you offer a course that shows people how to get a brain dead all of a sudden, I'll use Chris Lopez as another example. So if Chris is, if I'm doing this exercise and I'm trying to step step in the Chris's brain and just to refresh everybody's memory, Chris is a strength coach, strength, I'm calling him a guru, although I hate that term, strength coach, focusing primarily on using kettlebells, but just a very different philosophy. Just if you want to get strong, Chris is somebody who can get you strong. And Chris created a multi-page pre-sale site and an offer sometime last year that was really geared toward, I believe it was over 40 men. So I'm just going to riff on the over 40 men thing. I'm 49, I'm about to turn 50. So I'm just barely under the wire here. So if Chris is going through this exercise and he's thinking to himself, okay, what do they want? He could do this in a very cursory way, superficial way. Like, well, they want to get stronger and they want to feel good about themselves. Come on, yawn. Right? It's not just that. Like, yes, you want to be stronger. You want to look in the mirror and the person looking back doesn't have the gut sticking out and you've got the shoulders and the chins up, get the right posture, looks strong. And this is not something that the audience is probably willing to really say too much out loud. So you got to really get in their head. So there's a breadth of things that they want. They want to be able to go up a flight of stairs and not feel winded. They want to pick up, if they're in the 40s, they want to be able to maybe pick up a kid, depending on the age or whatever. But they want to be the strong dad around their kids. For example, if the market's primarily fathers, like you can make the list. Don't settle for they want to be stronger. That's not, who cares? Like get to the things they can do because they're stronger. Like the capabilities they have, those are the outcomes. And then you bring in the emotions. If you're physically strong, you're capable, you can do these things. How does that make you feel? And then just start, make your list at first and don't settle for the obvious. Think about it. What's it like to be, to have your, again, assuming these are over 40 dads, what's it like when your teenager or your almost teenager thinks dad is strong? How does that feel when dad's able to whatever, play all day with them? Or just go through the list. Like you're just trying to figure out really in depth, what is it they want and not in your, you're saying the things that they might not even admit themselves. And when you write a Facebook ad, just to be clear, a couple of things, even though we're talking to them, we don't want to use you or your variations of that. Facebook doesn't like that. Doesn't like us calling out another person like, Hey, you, so we want to stay away from that. But what we are still doing is we're, we're maintaining the focus entirely on them and what they're feeling and what it feels like when they try, what it feels like when they failed. Like we're setting up that world for them, those two poles between where they are and where they want to go. And we're, we're putting that language early in the ad because that's how we attract somebody. Right? That's early on in the ad. That's why we put it early in the ad is we're setting up and it's almost a frame within a frame because where the frame that we're initially starting here is that we're talking about them, not us. Right. And we're leading by talking about them. That says something subtly and often subconsciously. But if we start talking about ourselves and what we can do early, we're also saying something very different than if we start talking about them early. When we go from what do they want, and these two things, these are not sequential steps. You want them all there, I think in some kind of order like this, but they can blend to because what they want. But then another part we need to make sure that we have is how do they usually get that? Right. So you all continue using the strength example. There are some people and I don't want to throw stones at CrossFit, but it's fun. So why not? So there are some people who want to get in shape. They want to get stronger. And so they, they go to the CrossFit box, not even a gym, they go to the CrossFit box, three days a week, five days a week and wreck themselves. Right. So that's, you know, that's, that's a big commitment. And maybe they've become stronger, maybe they haven't. But if they're clicking on an ad about getting stronger, there's probably a pretty good chance they're not satisfied with the way they're doing it now. So you don't have to call out things by name. And I wouldn't call out things like CrossFit. I would call out the activities associated with the things that you're throwing stones at. So you could say something like, you know, if you're going to, if you're wrecking yourself in the gym five days a week, you can still not getting stronger. Like that's how I would phrase that or whatever it is in your market. What do people usually try to do to get what we've already established that they want, the real deep stuff. Right. So, and then as you're talking about that, you're talking about it in ways to show why that's not really working for them. And they already know it's not working for them. So you don't have to hammer them with it. But you can just point out the obvious stuff. Like how do they, how do you feel when you go to gym five days a week? Or what happens when you go, you're supposed to go to the gym five days a week and wreck it, but you only make it three days a week and you're not really getting any stronger anyway. Like that's, that's what we're opening up here. What this does is this does a very important, this has a very important role in the transition. We're, we're now introducing the possibility that it's the way they've been trying to get what they want. That's the problem. They're not the problem. And that is, it's a very subtle seed that we're planting early. Because if they're the problem, then, you know, then they just have to, it's, it's a, it's a sort of a downward spiral of, you know, like, okay, I've tried this and tried to do this a million times. I keep failing, I keep failing. It's just, I don't know what I'm doing. It's got to be me. And then if somebody comes along and says, well, actually, it's not, and we're not saying this overtly, we're being gentle about this. But what we're really saying is it's not necessarily you. That's the problem. It's the way you're trying to accomplish this, right? Maybe going to the gym and killing yourself five days a week, because that's what you learn to do when you're in college and you're a 20-something athlete. Like that may have made sense then. But now that you're in your 40s, it probably doesn't make as much sense. So maybe the problem is, the way you've been trying to do this is the issue, not you. Now they, there's some, like, the door has cracked a little bit at that point. That's when you're not literally seeing them nod their head up and down. But that is a point in early in the conversation where the right prospects pay a little more attention. And you've introduced a little bit of dissonance. They came in and thought, you know what, like, really what's happening is they clicked on this ad and they're thinking to themselves, you know, I'm, you know, why, why even bother? And they start reading and they're like, well, wait a second, this is kind of interesting. Like, it makes a lot of sense, right? You're getting them nod up and down a little bit. That's what you want the right prospects to do. This is the, this is again, this is a spectrum. These are not step by steps. But at this point, it's okay to highlight their frustrations gently. We're not trying to hammer their frustrations and rub salt in them and say, you know, it's just going to get worse and you're going to be old and dead before you know it. That's not what we're talking about here. But what we're now this is our chance to empathize because we're going to empathize and show credibility and we're bringing that together at the end in that order, the end of the ad versus bringing these things together. And this is when we become, we begin to have our voice in the ad is we're now reflecting back on them how somebody in their situation feels as a result. And that can be frustration. You can be kind of sad, you can be disappointed, disappointed in yourself. You can think, you're just, you're never going to figure it out, like why even bother, like whatever the language is, whatever you've heard people say, that's where we really, we get here. We don't, we don't want to crank the volume on their frustrations. Because this is, if you've noticed, this is proceeding very much like a conversation would and the best Facebook ads are conversational. So we've, instead of walking up to somebody and saying, Hey, let me, let me tell you about me and why I'm so great and all the things that I've done, that's not how most decent people behave. Right. What we do is you see somebody who is in some sort of pain or discomfort, right. And what do you do? You add like, Hey, what's, you know, what's going on for you? What is it you're trying to do here? Like, how does that make you feel a little bit of therapy here and do some, you know, not overtly, but that's what we're doing. Like, you know, what are you trying to do here? What had it like, how does that make you feel like, okay, well, you know, how have you been trying to do that? Oh, okay, right. Well, you know, be aware that, you know, some issues, the way you've been trying to do it, right? Nothing to do, nothing wrong with you. You didn't do anything wrong. But that may not be the best way to get what you want. Then when we transition, we talk about, but, you know, let's, let's take a step back here, because I can imagine, you know, let's just assume I'll be Chris again. Chris has met somebody or Chris is reaching out to some broadly speaking on a Facebook ad. And he he's effectively saying, Listen, I know what you want. And I know you don't like what you feel about like, or I know what you want. And I know how you want to feel as a result. Like I get it. Because really, that's Chris, he knows that it's he's worked with clients like that he's he is that so yeah, he knows that he knows the outcomes and the emotions. And I know there are 1000 different ways you could go about it. And you probably tried a bunch of them. And they all have their issues. And then you pick the top three or whatever you name the issues. And I get it that when you when you try to do things that, you know, they should work, right, this should, you know, there are plenty of people who say this is the right way to do it. And then you try it, and you don't get the result. And that, that's just miserable. Like now that's empathy. Right now you're saying like, Oh, like, I like, I feel your pain. And from that empathy, we transition to this idea of explaining a better way. And that's where we create hope and possibility. And that's where we establish authority, where we become the trusted guide. And that's when we invite them into our world. And that brings the add to a close, because we've covered all of the basis that we need to have an effective mini three minute ish conversation with an ideal person, because we've led by focusing on them, not us. We have, we have reflected back on them that we understand it. We've also reflected back some interest, we've, we've taken some interesting approaches here, because we have, when we throw stones at the big problems that creates an opportunity for us to be, to show our knowledge without ever drawing attention to ourselves as being knowledgeable, it's implied. So if, if I'm having this conversation with Chris, and I'm frustrated, I'm aging athlete, I want to get stronger, it's just not working. And when Chris listens to me, at first, I think he's just a good guy. But when he listens to me, and then he starts explaining through throwing stones at the way that I normally do this, I instinctively know that he must know something about this. And he never says it. He's commanding my respect rather than demanding it. He doesn't say, Hey, let me show you all of my credentials and everything I've done and everybody I've worked with. He's just simply giving me some feedback as an expert would, then he's moving on to empathy. So now he's drawn me closer to him. And it's not until the very end, where I actually have any idea about his actual authority, and he doesn't you don't have to overplay that at the end. That's not, it's not like wrap up your Facebook ad by saying, you know, here are the 10 reasons you should listen to me. It can be very conversational. You know, the for some somebody like Chris, he could say something like, you know, to establish authority and to show these a trusted guide, he could sort of bring it to a close by saying, you know, I absolutely understand the frustration that you're feeling. I've worked with hundreds, maybe thousands, I don't know the exact number, but let's just for the sake of argument, say Chris has worked with hundreds of people. So Chris could say, I absolutely understand your frustration. There are so many things that so many people do that should work yet they don't. And I've worked with hundreds of people, very similar situations. And what I've found is that there's actually three or four things that matter. I would, I would choose one. So what I found is there are actually three things that matter. And you can start explaining what you know, and you can sort of, it probably in this case, I would say, what I, you know, what I found is there are three things that matter. And, and then I would list them, you know, one, two, and three in the ad, don't make somebody go to a landing page to figure out, but also don't explain them. Yeah, I figured out there are three things that matter, one sentence, two, seven, three, seven, whatever. If you would like to learn more, click here. Or if you'd like to learn more, I explain this all I explain this in a three-party email series, you can find out the details here. Right. So now you've told them what's going to happen. They know they're going to something where there's going to be email involved. So if they don't ever opt in for anything, then you're filtering them out, which is great. But that's, that's a way at the end that you bring things to a close, and you have a call to action. And the call to action is the invi, it's the invitation into your world. And the invitation into your world is the final step that they make through the frame. Right. You have in the process of writing this ad by focusing on what they want, highlighting how they usually get it, drawing attention to their frustrations and explaining a better way. You have set this incredibly powerful frame, which is you are the person who understands this desire that they have. You know all of the issues with all the other ways that they've tried to get it. They know how they feel as a result. And you've actually done the work to know there's a better way. And the moment they click to find out what that better way is to take that step forward toward you, they have stepped completely into the frame that you have created for them. Now, this could be, depending on your market, there are different ways to go about this. So there's again, there's no, this is not a template. And this really isn't even a, I guess it is a framework. But what I want this to be is a way for all of you to think about how to write a Facebook ad with all four of these elements in this order, knowing that they'll mix together. There are no hacks. There's no, there's no template to follow. It is you having a conversation with somebody who you know is an ideal prospect. That's what this is all about. And it's going to be, when you try to do this, it's going to be frustrating. There's a couple of ways to get around it. One easy way to get around it is to have these prompts and then speak it like you're actually having a conversation with somebody because it'll change the dynamic a little bit. Stay away from you and your, don't talk to a an individual person, but you know, speak broadly. When you do that, it's going to seem a little clunky. And it's going to seem like, wait a minute, aren't we supposed to have this polished Facebook ad as part of the workshop? But what I'm doing here, normally I wouldn't draw attention to the trick here, but I'm going to draw attention to the trick. And trick is the wrong word, but just go with me. I have seen in my professional career, probably seven to 10 times, I have seen Facebook ads, I call them unicorn ads, that so outperform anything I've ever seen anywhere else. Sometimes in one case in particular, which is incredibly embarrassing, but it makes the point, which is I was working with a client and it was one of his competitors that the competitor ad destroyed anything that we could that we ever created. So we as the professionals, what we brought to the party got destroyed by this other person and who was not a professional, was not a Facebook advertiser. In fact, the ad was almost comical. Like I looked through, I thought you just can't be, this can't be right. But what it, what it had was that profound authenticity. And the moment I noticed that I began to realize I saw it everywhere, really high performing Facebook ads, very rarely, very, very rarely, seemed like Facebook ads. Some do in some markets, I mean, yes, there are some video ads, the, you know, the squatty potty stuff for the world, like, sure, there are some outliers that are professional and slick and polished, and they make a good $1 trillion, sure. But in my experience, the ads that I've seen that really, really outperform don't seem like ads. They seem like somebody just tried to explain to another person, like a human being, how they could help. And what I've tried to accomplish here is to give you enough of the guardrails that you can write a unicorn ad. And it sounds crazy. I know it sounds crazy, because you're thinking to yourself, but like, how am I supposed to do that? But the reality is, it has nothing to do with writing Facebook ads, and it has everything to do with your expertise. And we don't want anything else to get in the way. So follow these four parts. Sit down, you can screenshot this, I would prefer that you write them in longhand. After you've written the list in longhand, and write all of all four prompts, maybe on four different sheets of paper, if that's helpful for you, just get it out of your head and ask why over and over again and ask, why does that matter? Why does that matter? Who cares? Like, ask yourself these questions, but get something in each of these columns. And once you have something in each of these columns, sit down and write the ad start to finish, like you're having a record it, like you're having a conversation with somebody. And just go through this process. Talk to them about what they want. Talk about the outcomes. Talk about how they feel about it. How do they want to feel? Like just like you would with a good friend, or one of your ideal customers. And then just go through each part and write it and don't try to polish it. Just write it. And a couple of things to keep in mind with Facebook ads, you know, have more white space, so don't do seven sentence paragraphs and stuff. You know, just one or two lines, have a little bit of rhythm, like it's like you're talking, read it out loud. But go through these parts, write it, start to finish, like you're talking to another human being. And if you can do that, I suspect that you would beat many, possibly all of the really highly polished Facebook ads, because Facebook is a social environment. It is a conversational environment. And when you tap into that social conversational tone, you go underneath the defenses of your audience because they're expecting the polish, they're expecting the ad. And instead, you're treating them like a human being. And ultimately, that's what matters. And for those people who don't like that, who opt out early, excellent, they're not going to be your happy customers. What we want are the people who resonate with that tone that you've created. And at the end of the day, are willing to take that step forward into this journey with you into a frame that you have established that is going to be the boundaries of the reality, of their reality for the entirety of that journey.
